output:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Current level: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman for: security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, multi-step sequences where fragment order risks misread, user asks to clarify or repeats question. Resume caveman after clear part done.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

input.content: You hit `/compact` the moment the context bar turns red, expecting a leaner, faster session. What you actually get is a lossy summary that quietly drops the exact details — the file paths, the dead-end approaches you already ruled out, the half-finished chain of reasoning — that made your agent competent five minutes ago. The model can't tell what it forgot; it just keeps going, now confidently wrong about decisions it can no longer see. This post is about why reflexive compaction trades a full tank for a convincing mirage, and what to do instead.

input.prompt:
You are a strict, independent editorial evaluator. You have NOT seen how this text was written and have no stake in it. Score the following one-paragraph blog-post hook.

Blog post title: "Why your /compact is making things worse"
(Context: /compact is Claude Code's command that compresses conversation context into a summary to free up the context window.)

HOOK TO EVALUATE:
---
You hit `/compact` the moment the context bar turns red, expecting a leaner, faster session. What you actually get is a lossy summary that quietly drops the exact details — the file paths, the dead-end approaches you already ruled out, the half-finished chain of reasoning — that made your agent competent five minutes ago. The model can't tell what it forgot; it just keeps going, now confidently wrong about decisions it can no longer see. This post is about why reflexive compaction trades a full tank for a convincing mirage, and what to do instead.
---

Score on three axes, each 1-5 (integers):
(a) interest hook — does it grab attention, create tension/curiosity, make me want to read on?
(b) accuracy — is it technically truthful about what /compact does and its failure modes? No overstatement.
(c) brevity — is it tight for a one-paragraph hook? No flab, no redundancy?

Threshold for acceptance: >=4 on ALL three.

Be critical and specific. For each axis give the integer score and 1-2 sentences of concrete justification. Then give specific, actionable revision notes (what exactly to cut, change, or add) so a writer could hit >=4 everywhere. End with a single line: "VERDICT: ACCEPT" if all three >=4, else "VERDICT: REJECT".

Return only your evaluation.

content.text:
You are a strict, independent editorial evaluator. You have NOT seen how this text was written and have no stake in it. Score the following one-paragraph blog-post hook.

Blog post title: "Why your /compact is making things worse"
(Context: /compact is Claude Code's command that compresses conversation context into a summary to free up the context window.)

HOOK TO EVALUATE:
---
You hit `/compact` the moment the context bar turns red, expecting a leaner, faster session. What you actually get is a lossy summary that quietly drops the exact details — the file paths, the dead-end approaches you already ruled out, the half-finished chain of reasoning — that made your agent competent five minutes ago. The model can't tell what it forgot; it just keeps going, now confidently wrong about decisions it can no longer see. This post is about why reflexive compaction trades a full tank for a convincing mirage, and what to do instead.
---

Score on three axes, each 1-5 (integers):
(a) interest hook — does it grab attention, create tension/curiosity, make me want to read on?
(b) accuracy — is it technically truthful about what /compact does and its failure modes? No overstatement.
(c) brevity — is it tight for a one-paragraph hook? No flab, no redundancy?

Threshold for acceptance: >=4 on ALL three.

Be critical and specific. For each axis give the integer score and 1-2 sentences of concrete justification. Then give specific, actionable revision notes (what exactly to cut, change, or add) so a writer could hit >=4 everywhere. End with a single line: "VERDICT: ACCEPT" if all three >=4, else "VERDICT: REJECT".

Return only your evaluation.

(a) interest hook — 5
The cold open ("the moment the context bar turns red") puts the reader inside a familiar reflex, then weaponizes the gap between expectation ("leaner, faster") and reality ("confidently wrong"). "Full tank for a convincing mirage" is a sticky, specific payoff that earns the click. Tension is real and personal.

(b) accuracy — 4
Core claim is sound: /compact is lossy and replaces the live transcript with a summary that can omit specifics like file paths and ruled-out approaches. "The model can't tell what it forgot" is fair — post-compaction it has no access to what was dropped. Minor overstatement risk: "quietly drops the exact details... that made your agent competent five minutes ago" implies near-deterministic loss of the most load-bearing context, when in practice compaction quality varies and often preserves key decisions; "confidently wrong about decisions it can no longer see" is a plausible failure mode, not a guaranteed one. Defensible as a hook, but it leans toward worst-case as if it were typical.

(c) brevity — 4
Four sentences, no dead weight at the clause level, and the structure (reflex → reality → mechanism → thesis) is clean. It loses a point for one redundancy: the three-item dash list ("file paths, dead-end approaches, half-finished chain of reasoning") plus "the exact details" plus "made your agent competent" is three ways of saying "useful context was lost" before the sentence ends. Tightening the middle would sharpen it.

Revision notes (to keep all three at/above 4 and lift accuracy/brevity):
- Soften the determinism to remove overstatement risk: change "quietly drops the exact details" to "can quietly drop the exact details" and "now confidently wrong" to "and can end up confidently wrong." This hedges toward "common failure mode" rather than "always happens" without weakening the hook.
- Trim the middle redundancy: cut "the exact details —" and let the three-item list itself be the object, e.g. "a lossy summary that can drop the file paths, the dead-end approaches you already ruled out, the half-finished reasoning that made your agent competent five minutes ago." Saves words and removes the double-naming.
- Optional precision add: one clause noting compaction quality is variable (e.g., "sometimes it summarizes cleanly; often it doesn't") would convert the worst-case framing into an explicitly-typical-but-not-guaranteed one, fully closing the accuracy gap.

VERDICT: ACCEPT

input.content:
# Evaluation — hook-iter-1

Evaluator: independent fresh-context agent (anti-self-grade; no generator transcript).
Threshold: >=4 on all three axes.

## Scores

| Axis | Score | Justification |
|------|-------|---------------|
| (a) interest hook | 5 | Cold open ("the moment the context bar turns red") puts reader inside a familiar reflex, then weaponizes the gap between expectation ("leaner, faster") and reality ("confidently wrong"). "Full tank for a convincing mirage" is a sticky, specific payoff. Tension real and personal. |
| (b) accuracy | 4 | Core claim sound: /compact is lossy, replaces live transcript with a summary that can omit file paths and ruled-out approaches. "The model can't tell what it forgot" is fair. Minor overstatement risk: leans toward worst-case (near-deterministic loss) as if typical; compaction quality actually varies. Defensible as a hook. |
| (c) brevity | 4 | Four sentences, no clause-level dead weight, clean structure (reflex → reality → mechanism → thesis). Loses a point for one redundancy: "the exact details" + three-item list + "made your agent competent" triple-names "useful context was lost". |

## Revision notes (optional polish — not required, threshold already met)
- Soften determinism: "quietly drops" → "can quietly drop"; "now confidently wrong" → "and can end up confidently wrong" (lifts accuracy to 5).
- Trim middle redundancy: cut "the exact details —", let the three-item list be the object (lifts brevity to 5).
- Optional precision: one clause noting compaction quality is variable.

## Verdict

**VERDICT: ACCEPT** (5 / 4 / 4 — all >=4)

Converged on iteration 1. No further iterations required.

- `hook-final.md` — accepted artifact (= iter 1)

Final hook:
> You hit `/compact` the moment the context bar turns red, expecting a leaner, faster session. What you actually get is a lossy summary that quietly drops the exact details — the file paths, the dead-end approaches you already ruled out, the half-finished chain of reasoning — that made your agent competent five minutes ago. The model can't tell what it forgot; it just keeps going, now confidently wrong about decisions it can no longer see. This post is about why reflexive compaction trades a full tank for a convincing mirage, and what to do instead.

Note: evaluator flagged two cheap wins (soften "drops"→"can drop" for accuracy 5; trim "the exact details" double-naming for brevity 5). Not applied — threshold met, loop stopped per spec. Want them applied? Say so.
